title: La famille
description: Family vocabulary + possessive adjectives (mon, ma, mes).

cards.notes: Note the **œ** ligature — it's one letter, pronounced /œ/ (no English equivalent; like the eu in *peur*).
Pronounced **"feece"** — silent l, the s is voiced. Watch for the spelling, which doesn't reflect the sound.
Same word for *daughter* and *girl* — context decides.
Starts with a vowel — elided. *l'oncle*, not *le oncle*.
Always plural in this sense. Also means *relatives* in some contexts (*mes parents éloignés* — my distant relatives).
Used before masculine singular nouns: *mon père*, *mon frère*. ALSO used before any feminine noun that starts with a vowel: *mon amie* (not *ma amie*). The elision rule from Lesson 03 strikes again.
Used before feminine singular nouns that start with a CONSONANT: *ma mère*, *ma sœur*, *ma tante*. Before a vowel, switches to *mon*.
Same form for masculine and feminine plurals: *mes parents*, *mes sœurs*.

steps.title: The family tree
steps.body:
# La famille — family vocabulary

Family words come in matched gender pairs, just like in English (father / mother, son / daughter). French adds the same article system you learned in Lesson 03:

- *le père* — the father
- *la mère* — the mother
- *les parents* — the parents (always plural)
- *le frère* / *la sœur* — brother / sister
- *le fils* / *la fille* — son / daughter
- *l'oncle* / *la tante* — uncle / aunt (note the elision on *oncle*)

This lesson adds **possessive adjectives** (*mon*, *ma*, *mes* — my) so you can talk about YOUR family, not just family in the abstract.

exercise.prompt: Match each French family word to its English meaning.

steps.title: Mon, ma, mes — my
steps.body:
## Possessive adjectives

French possessives agree with the **thing owned**, not with the owner. "My father" uses *mon* because *père* is masculine — even if a woman says it.

| Form | When |
|---|---|
| **mon** | m. singular: *mon père*, *mon frère*, *mon livre* |
| **ma** | f. singular, consonant start: *ma mère*, *ma sœur*, *ma maison* |
| **mes** | any plural: *mes parents*, *mes sœurs* |

Key trap: before a feminine noun starting with a **vowel**, French uses **mon** instead of *ma* — because *ma amie* would be ugly to pronounce. So:

- *mon amie* (my friend, f.) — NOT *ma amie*
- *mon école* (my school, f.) — NOT *ma école*

Same rule as the article elision in Lesson 03, just applied to the possessive.

exercise.hint: Possessive agrees with the noun's gender.

exercise.prompt: *amie* (friend, f.) starts with a vowel. Which possessive is correct?
exercise.hint: Feminine + vowel start → use the masculine-shape *mon* to avoid the awkward *ma amie*.

steps.title: Describing relationships
steps.body:
## Putting it together

With family words + possessives + the verbs from Lesson 04, you can describe your family in real sentences:

- *J'ai **un frère et deux sœurs**.* — I have one brother and two sisters.
- ***Mon père est** médecin.* — My father is a doctor.
- ***Ma sœur a** dix ans.* — My sister is 10.
- ***Mes parents habitent** à Lyon.* — My parents live in Lyon.
- ***Mon oncle est** français.* — My uncle is French.

Notice the verbs you already know: *avoir* for possession (one brother), *être* for identity (a doctor), *habiter* for location.

Building sentences out of pieces you've already learned is how A1 starts feeling fluent.

exercise.prompt: Arrange to mean *I have two brothers and one sister*.
exercise.hint: Plural brothers, singular sister. Indefinite article for *one sister*.

exercise.prompt: Match each noun with the correct possessive form of *my*.

steps.title: Build: My brother is a teacher

exercise.prompt: Arrange to mean *My brother is a teacher*. (Remember: no article before a profession.)
